Aloha everyone. I'm Breck, a researcher at the UH Cancer Center Honolulu. My main communities these days are HN, Lobsters, /r/programminglanguages/, and SUS. I work on Tree Notation and am creating the Tree Notation Lab here. Our theory is there is a natural notation sitting between binary notation and higher level languages that has been overlooked and it could be a silver bullet. We call it Tree Notation and it's very promising (though we might have some details wrong---always looking for help). To be thorough, we build the largest database of programming and computer languages in the world with over 10K languages and over 1K columns (in the process of open sourcing it!). The first key applications we are working on is for a new data science language and studio for cancer/bioinformatics researchers, and a new grammar for electronic medical records. We are also helping over 30 companies now experiment with new languages in domains from tax returns to logic languages to visual ides and new languages for deep learning. DC referred me to this community and it looks very cool! I have never gotten into the Slack habit, so I apologize for noob mistakes.
If anyone is visiting Oahu and wants to grab a coffee at the cancer center, shoot me an email <mailto:breck7@gmail.com|breck7@gmail.com> or <mailto:yunits@hawaii.edu|yunits@hawaii.edu>
I'm curious about Tree Notation. How does it compare to something like, say, LLVM IR?
You could make a Tree Language that compiles to LLVM IR
Tree Notation is a dumb, error-less notation (like binary), that is just a slight step above binary, giving you trees, abstraction and scopes
Although dead simple, it has very interesting properties. It makes programs isomorphic to spreadsheets (instead of function arguments, for example, we have cells fixed in geometry)
It plays nicely with git -- all changes are semantic and nodes are line based
I could ramble on all day about it

But hopefully soon the tooling, demos, and docs will do a better job of explaining it than I can....(I've found it's actually often more helpful if someone else explains it to people, as I've been working on it for 7 years I forget what it's like to see it with fresh eyes)

attachments.title: An Ode to Excel: 34 Years of Magic :sparkles:
attachments.text: In an age where "software is eating the world", what can we learn from the tool that has withstood the test of time? This piece illustrates how the fundamentals behind Excel can be used to envision the next wave of bulletproof technologies.

If every tree is valid, and no syntax errors can happen, then what is to prevent a programmer from making logical mistakes? Isn't the whole progression of computer languages been one of a steady march towards having the computer do more of the detail work so as to reduce the chance for human error, which is the main thing we fight in computers. Machines are incredibly fast and have large storage now, the only thing limiting us is our ability to make reliable programs. Sorry, but the tree notation seems formless and thus not in the direction of progress. Change  my mind...
If you're new to Slack, feel free to send me a Direct Message with any questions and I'd be happy to help.
If you're new to Slack, feel free to send me a Direct Message with any questions and I'd be happy to help.

Excel is a very successful product, but it isn't perfect, and there were many innovations on the way that have not received proper credit. Excel is now a fairly bloated product and i think successive versions are getting harder to use. The spreadsheet does have many fundamental features worth copying, and i have done so in my Beads language: 1) protected, extended arithmetic 2) automatic natural order of recalculation/refresh, 3) no object creation, all things already exist already, they just might not have a value yet

And  a considerable part of Excel's success is due to marketing prowess and the overwhelming power of Microsoft. There have been many innovative products superior to Excel in different ways that never got much traction.
This has probably been discussed before here, but I'd love to hear any recommendations from people here for the spreadsheet state of the art today
One should always distinguish between popularity and actual merit. Van Gogh sold one painting in his life.

I've never used Excel very much, but one of the things that's always intrigued me about it is that it has base of very passionate users. It feels like a product many people truly enjoy using, there aren't a lot of software products that genuinely garner affection in that way
Between the twin jaws of "free" (google), and pushed by 500+ billion dollar market cap company (MS), no competing product has any chance of surviving. Only crumbs left.
Airtable is doing very well as a mishmash of Excel and Access
For competitors off the top of my head there's Google Sheets, Airtable, OpenOffice, iWork Numbers, any other big ones?

On a tangent, I _love_ the idea of mentioning favorite forums/watering holes in one's introduction. Can densely convey a lot of intangible information to just the right people. I used to do it a few years ago, but fell off the wagon. Thanks for the reminder!

Hi Breck! We've chatted (and :crossed_swords: ) before, happy to see you here.

What is SUS, and what is DC? :slightly_smiling_face:

I would argue that AirTable is an entirely different kind of product than a spreadsheet, which is a very specific tool like a hammer. I view AirTable as a collaborative database with various modules, some of which resemble spreadsheets. One could argue that it is merger of those two worlds (spreadsheet and database) that has not been seen much since the very old Framework product that Robert Carr wrote in the 80's.  Framework got so hot they did TV advertising for it; when was the last time you saw a TV commercial for consumer software?

Sorry <@UHWC9PXBL> the sandbox link changed to <http://treenotation.org/designer/>. We need to setup a linkator test job to make sure we identify broken links

Hi <@UEQ6M68H0>. There are no errors at the Tree Notation level, just as there is no such thing as an error in binary notation. However, there are errors at the "Tree Language" level. If you try some of the sample languages in the designer app (you might need a stiff drink--the app is still early stages), you can make an error and see how because Tree Languages are so much easier to parse since the AST == Source Code and there's no visible syntax characters, we can create a whole new generation of more intelligent editing experiences. Right now, depending on the type of error, we are able to suggest autocorrections immediately. I 100% agree with you in the importance of reliability. I expect we see a reduction in software defects of 10x+ in Tree Projects vs current projects across the industry in a few years. Once you have put a software project in the minimal Tree form, it's done and timeless. The parts of our software stack that have the fewest bugs are all in Tree Languages now.

text: Hi <@UMPQ9A65N> Sounds cool! I've made syntax highlighters for Sublime (which is basically Textmate's format in YAML) and it has been really painful experience. Mainly because my languages are context sensitive. I've also made them in CodeMirror, and it's a total breeze. All I need is access to the document, and can easily spit out hightlight scopes for any position programmatically if I can run my own parser code. Will the new thing you are working on allow me to run my own JS code to return highlights? It sounds like it, but I just want to confirm

<@UMVFWPZ36> and Kartik, I’m very interested in what you learned about “how to research properly”, though it’s a tangent off a tangent now. I feel like that might be the most important thing I didn’t learn by not getting a PhD.

<@UD3AK9JRF> I don't have a phd either. I was able to get a job at UHCC because they needed people who knew cloud computing and machine learning. I didn't know biology but the team knows that (I've learned a lot but still far from an expert) and it's been a great partnership. I've worked alongside lots of bioinformaticians, researchers, and clinicians analyzing data and writing code ; been coauthor on 10 or so peer reviewed papers now (a few still in the review process) whereas before I couldn't define "peer review" ; I've been on the reviewer side of the table; have gone to the academic conferences and talks; gotten to know lots of academics and got some great mentors. So I think I at least now "know how to research properly" (thought I reserve my right to do it in a slightly different way :wink: ) which has been helpful. I also just doubled down heavily on getting better at building huge curated datasets, because data trumps brains :slightly_smiling_face:.
Thanks, Breck. What from that experience has been particularly useful in your FoC work? It also sounds like you’ve been involved in that academic work for a while, did you know Kartik beforehand?
Just from around the web.
